# Source Card: Development of a WHO growth reference for school-aged children and adolescents

## Summary
This peer-reviewed WHO methods paper (de Onis et al., Bull World Health Organ 2007;85(9):660-667) documents construction of the WHO 2007 growth reference for the 5-19 years age group (height-for-age, weight-for-age, BMI-for-age), built with the Box-Cox power exponential (BCPE) method to align with the 0-5y WHO Child Growth Standards at age 5 and with adult BMI cutoffs at 19 years. For a pediatric CDS growth module it establishes the anchor SD-score BMI values at 19 years (+1 SD equated to adult overweight >=25 kg/m2; +2 SD compared with adult obesity >=30 kg/m2), the smooth 5-year transition, and the scope limits of each indicator (weight-for-age only to 10 years; height/BMI-for-age to 19 years). It is source-supported evidence for reference selection, not an executable classification rule.

## Key evidence (with exact-passage locators)
- (ev_001) +1 SD BMI-for-age at 19y = 25.4 kg/m2 (boys) / 25.0 kg/m2 (girls), equated to adult overweight >=25.0 kg/m2 — locator: Abstract/Results/Discussion — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:aed51cdf48be8cf774b1457ea1f31ef956fcf89a7d5ba32015946a793104bd87]"
- (ev_002) +2 SD BMI-for-age at 19y = 29.7 kg/m2 (both sexes), compared with adult obesity >=30.0 kg/m2 — locator: Abstract/Discussion — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:7ab8e48de8eb90228012eb7807050d5dea7a83864bf0e0e64987771bef80417f]"
- (ev_003) BMI-for-age transition at 5y differs mostly 0.0-0.1 kg/m2, never >0.2 kg/m2 — locator: Results/Abstract — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:fd8845ba81792b803fe3f16446640d59c90839a4162b2ecfb5cdcfc98862fdc5]"
- (ev_004) Weight-for-age charts extend only to 10 years (inadequate beyond childhood) — locator: Discussion — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:a11e8a7de5eeead3f7cda97b4d8b9c4d33d59e22bdd080fbf218aaa7096b1c5f]"
- (ev_005) Height-for-age and BMI-for-age charts extend to 19 years (5-19y reference) — locator: Discussion/Abstract — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:4ef962808e4621350e1286a43e4827e8daeb8b54d05f4626d14985549cc82e14]"
- (ev_006) Curves built with the BCPE method (same as 0-5y standards) — locator: Methods — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:38ec4748bf222fbad9d6b277b6d30015f7bfd87f036a56a99e9e267186e314b4]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Population: school-aged children and adolescents, 5-19 years; BMI/height cutoffs are sex-specific and quoted at exactly 19 years; the 5y point is the transition boundary with the WHO 0-5y Child Growth Standards.
- Assay/method: all thresholds are anthropometric (BMI = weight/height^2) and statistically derived (SD-scores via BCPE), NOT analyzer/assay-dependent — no method-specific reference intervals apply.
- Lifecycle: WHO 2007 reference; supersedes the NCHS/WHO 1977 reference (1-24y) for this age band; anchored to adult BMI cutoffs (overweight >=25, obesity >=30 kg/m2). No retire date stated in-source.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- Indicator-scope boundary (not a clinical red-flag): weight-for-age is declared inadequate beyond childhood and its charts stop at 10 years; height-for-age and BMI-for-age are the indicators carried to 19 years. This bounds which growth indicator a CDS module may use at a given age. The source defines no diagnosis/treatment/referral trigger.

## Limitations & conflicts
- The +1/+2 SD BMI values (25.4/25.0 and 29.7 kg/m2) are anchor values AT 19 YEARS ONLY; the full age/sex-specific cutoff tables and L/M/S parameters live in the WHO reference tables/charts, which are NOT reproduced in this paper — treat per-age thresholds as GAP: the age-by-age BMI-for-age cutoff table is not locatable in this source (source = de Onis 2007 methods paper); consult the WHO 2007 reference tables for operational per-age cutoffs.
- The reference is derived from the 1977 NCHS/WHO US-based sample merged with the 0-5y standards sample; it is descriptive/reference (not prescriptive like the 0-5y standards). Potential conflict with other adolescent references (e.g. IOTF/Cole cutoffs, CDC 2000, or national references) is not resolved here and must be preserved as a distinct reference choice, never averaged.
- Exact numeric per-centile transition differences beyond the "mostly 0.0-0.1, never >0.2 kg/m2" summary are not tabulated in the accessed text.
